comments:
Summary:
This paper addresses pedestrian detection by (1) introducing spatially pooled variants of commonly used low-level features (covariance descriptors and LBP) and (2) proposing a structured- learning based ensemble method (pAUCEnsT) that directly optimizes the partial area under the ROC curve (pAUC) over an arbitrary false-positive-rate interval [α, β]. The detector pipeline also uses BING region proposals to accelerate scan-time evaluation. The method is evaluated on multiple benchmarks (INRIA, ETH, TUD-Brussels, Caltech, KITTI) and on synthetic/bioinformatics tasks; experiments show strong empirical performance and claimed state-of-the-art results at the time.

Strengths:
- The paper tackles a practically important evaluation metric (pAUC) and develops a principled structured-learning ensemble that explicitly optimizes it. Building such an ensemble (boosting-like) from structured SVM principles is a useful contribution.
- The spatial pooling treatment of covariance and LBP features (sp-Cov, sp-LBP) is a pragmatic way to increase robustness while keeping computational costs lower than full dictionary learning pipelines.
- The manuscript contains a clear algorithmic description (cutting-plane solver, column generation for weak learner selection) and provides convergence analysis bounds.
- Extensive empirical evaluation across synthetic, bioinformatics, and multiple pedestrian detection benchmarks, including ablation/variants and baseline comparisons, supporting the claims.
- The paper is largely well organized and positions itself relative to prior work (pAUC SVMs, asymmetric boosting, recent detectors).

Weaknesses and concerns / suggestions:
- Computational trade-offs could be more thoroughly discussed. Training complexity is significant (authors mention up to 24 hours with parallel cores), and the detector increases scanning time compared with fast cascades. The paper should report full end-to-end timings that include feature extraction and post-processing for fairer deployment comparison. It would help readers to have memory/CPU requirements and wall-clock training times for representative settings.
- The choice and sensitivity of the pAUC interval [α, β] is central but only briefly discussed. More guidance or experiments showing sensitivity to β (and α when non-zero) would be valuable because practitioners must choose these for specific applications.
- While many baselines are included, comparison to then-recent deep-learning based detectors (beyond ConvNet citation) could be expanded (although timing/feature differences make direct comparisons difficult). The paper mentions outperforming several complex detectors, but more details (which exact variants, training data, and protocols) would help reproducibility.
- Some methodological details that affect reproducibility are only lightly described: precise pooling region sizes for each dataset, how hyperparameters (ν, shrinkage, BING thresholds) were selected, and whether code/models will be released. A dedicated subsection with full training hyperparameters would strengthen the work.
- The text contains a few editorial issues (repetitions, minor typographical errors; e.g., author email domain misspelling in the header) which should be cleaned.

Recommendation and final remarks:
Overall, this is a solid and practically relevant paper with a principled algorithmic contribution (structured ensemble optimizing pAUC) and complementary feature engineering (spatial pooling for covariance and LBP). The experimental evidence is strong and diverse. I recommend acceptance / wide dissemination (high score). The main improvements to consider are clearer presentation of hyperparameters and reproducibility details, more discussion of runtime trade-offs and sensitivity studies on the pAUC interval, and minor editorial cleanup.
